topics.title: Joins and Subqueries
topics.description: JOINs combine data from multiple tables — the most powerful SQL feature. Subqueries let you nest queries inside queries for complex filtering. Together they solve 95% of multi-table data problems.
topics.story: A company has two separate filing cabinets: one for employees (names, salaries, dept_id), one for departments (dept_id, dept_name). To print payslips you need data from both. A JOIN is the act of opening both cabinets simultaneously and matching records by the dept_id key — like paper-clipping related documents together.
topics.analogy: INNER JOIN = Venn diagram center (only rows with matches on BOTH sides). LEFT JOIN = left circle + center (all left rows, NULLs where right has no match). Think of LEFT JOIN as 'show me all employees even if their department was deleted.'
topics.memoryAnchor: JOIN types: INNER (intersection), LEFT (left + intersection), RIGHT (right + intersection), FULL OUTER (union). Subqueries: use IN for lists, EXISTS for existence check, correlated subquery uses outer query's value.
topics.firstPrinciples: A JOIN creates a Cartesian product (every row paired with every row) then filters by the ON condition. INNER JOIN keeps only matched pairs. LEFT JOIN adds unmatched left rows with NULLs on the right. The ON condition links the tables via foreign key relationships.
topics.bruteForce: Without JOINs: fetch all employees in one query, all departments in another, then loop and match in application code — O(n*m) application-side work, n+m round trips. JOINs push this work to the database engine which uses indexes and optimized algorithms (hash join, merge join, nested loop).
topics.optimizedApproach: Index foreign key columns (dept_id). Use INNER JOIN when you only care about matched rows (smaller result). Use EXISTS instead of IN for large subquery results (EXISTS short-circuits). Use CTEs for readable multi-step queries instead of nested subqueries.
topics.whenToUse: INNER JOIN: rows that exist in both tables. LEFT JOIN: all rows from left + optionally matched from right (find missing data: customers with no orders). Subquery with IN: filter by a dynamic list. Correlated subquery: per-row computation using outer query values. EXISTS: check if related rows exist.

examples.title: INNER JOIN — Only Matching Rows
examples.code:
-- Match employees to their departments
-- Employees without a department are EXCLUDED
SELECT
    e.name        AS employee,
    e.salary,
    d.name        AS department
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d
    ON e.dept_id = d.id
ORDER BY d.name, e.salary DESC;
examples.tableData: {"type":"join","joinType":"INNER","joinKey":{"left":"dept_id","right":"id"},"leftTable":{"name":"employees","columns":["id","name","salary","dept_id"],"rows":[[1,"Alice",85000,10],[2,"Bob",55000,20],[3,"Carol",92000,10],[4,"Dave",70000,99]]},"rightTable":{"name":"departments","columns":["id","name"],"rows":[[10,"Engineering"],[20,"Marketing"],[30,"HR"]]},"matchedRows":[[0,0],[1,1],[2,0]],"leftUnmatched":[3],"rightUnmatched":[2],"result":{"columns":["employee","salary","department"],"rows":[["Carol",92000,"Engineering"],["Alice",85000,"Engineering"],["Bob",55000,"Marketing"]]},"explanation":"Dave (dept_id=99) excluded — no dept with id=99. HR department (id=30) excluded — no employees in it. Only matched pairs survive INNER JOIN."}
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":7,"lineCode":"FROM employees e INNER JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id","variables":{"step":"Cartesian product + filter"},"phase":"JOIN","annotation":"Database engine pairs every employee with every department (12 combinations), then keeps only those where dept_id=id. 3 matched pairs."},{"line":7,"lineCode":"Dave (dept_id=99)","variables":{"dept_id":99,"match":"none"},"phase":"EXCLUDE","annotation":"Dave's dept_id=99 matches no department. INNER JOIN excludes him entirely."},{"line":7,"lineCode":"HR department (id=30)","variables":{"dept":"HR","employees":"none"},"phase":"EXCLUDE","annotation":"HR has no employees. INNER JOIN excludes it entirely."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"ORDER BY d.name, e.salary DESC","variables":{"result":"Engineering(Carol 92k, Alice 85k), Marketing(Bob 55k)"},"phase":"ORDER","annotation":"Sort: Engineering first (alphabetical), within dept: highest salary first."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A["employees 4 rows"] --> C["INNER JOIN ON dept_id=id"]
    B["departments 3 rows"] --> C
    C --> D["12 candidate pairs"]
    D --> E["Filter: dept_id MUST = id"]
    E --> F["3 matched pairs"]
    E --> G["Dave excluded (dept 99 missing)"]
    E --> H["HR excluded (no employees)"]
    F --> I["Result: 3 rows"]
examples.pseudocode:
INNER JOIN mental model:
1. Cartesian product: every employee × every department
2. Keep only pairs where ON condition is true
3. Discard all unmatched rows from BOTH sides

When to use INNER JOIN:
- You only want data that has a match on both sides
- 'Show me employees WITH a department'
- 'Show me orders WITH a customer'

Inner join = intersection (Venn diagram center only)
examples.explanation: INNER JOIN returns rows only where there's a match in BOTH tables. Dave (dept_id=99) is excluded because no department has id=99. HR is excluded because no employee belongs to it. This is the most common join type — use it when you need data from both tables and incomplete matches are irrelevant.
examples.realWorldUse: E-commerce: orders with product details (every order has a product). Analytics: user activity with user profiles. HR: employees with their department info. Whenever you need to enrich one table's data with related data from another table, and you don't care about records without a match.

examples.title: LEFT JOIN — All Left Rows, NULL Where No Match
examples.code:
-- Show ALL employees, even those with no department
-- (NULL in dept column means orphaned record)
SELECT
    e.name        AS employee,
    e.salary,
    d.name        AS department,   -- NULL if no matching dept
    d.budget
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d
    ON e.dept_id = d.id;
examples.tableData: {"type":"join","joinType":"LEFT","joinKey":{"left":"dept_id","right":"id"},"leftTable":{"name":"employees","columns":["id","name","salary","dept_id"],"rows":[[1,"Alice",85000,10],[2,"Bob",55000,20],[3,"Carol",92000,10],[4,"Dave",70000,99]]},"rightTable":{"name":"departments","columns":["id","name","budget"],"rows":[[10,"Engineering",500000],[20,"Marketing",200000],[30,"HR",150000]]},"matchedRows":[[0,0],[1,1],[2,0]],"leftUnmatched":[3],"rightUnmatched":[2],"result":{"columns":["employee","salary","department","budget"],"rows":[["Alice",85000,"Engineering",500000],["Bob",55000,"Marketing",200000],["Carol",92000,"Engineering",500000],["Dave",70000,null,null]]},"explanation":"Dave's dept_id=99 has no match → department=NULL, budget=NULL. But Dave IS included (LEFT JOIN keeps ALL left rows). HR is still excluded — it has no employees."}
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":8,"lineCode":"FROM employees e LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id","variables":{"left_rows":4,"result_rows":4},"phase":"JOIN","annotation":"LEFT JOIN: all 4 employees are guaranteed to appear in the result."},{"line":8,"lineCode":"Alice, Bob, Carol","variables":{"match":true},"phase":"MATCH","annotation":"Alice(dept_id=10→Engineering), Bob(dept_id=20→Marketing), Carol(dept_id=10→Engineering). All 3 match. Normal join rows."},{"line":8,"lineCode":"Dave (dept_id=99)","variables":{"match":false,"result":"NULL for right-side columns"},"phase":"NO_MATCH","annotation":"Dave's dept_id=99 has no match. LEFT JOIN keeps Dave but fills department and budget with NULL."},{"line":1,"lineCode":"HR (id=30) still excluded","variables":{"note":"LEFT means keep LEFT table rows, not RIGHT"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"HR has no employees — it's on the RIGHT side. LEFT JOIN guarantees left rows, not right rows."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A["employees 4 rows\n(guaranteed in result)"] --> C["LEFT JOIN"]
    B["departments 3 rows"] --> C
    C --> D["Alice → Engineering ✓"]
    C --> E["Bob → Marketing ✓"]
    C --> F["Carol → Engineering ✓"]
    C --> G["Dave → NULL (dept 99 missing) ✓ still included"]
    C --> H["HR → excluded (no employees)"]
examples.pseudocode:
LEFT JOIN mental model:
1. Start with ALL rows from the LEFT table
2. For each left row, try to find a match in the right table
3. If match found: include right columns normally
4. If NO match: include left row with NULL for all right columns

Key use case — find records with NO match:
  SELECT e.name FROM employees e
  LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id
  WHERE d.id IS NULL  -- employees with orphaned dept_id

This pattern (LEFT JOIN + WHERE right.id IS NULL)
= 'find left rows with no matching right row'
examples.explanation: LEFT JOIN preserves ALL rows from the left (first) table. Right-side columns show NULL for unmatched rows. The most powerful use: find orphaned records — `LEFT JOIN + WHERE right.pk IS NULL` finds left rows with NO match (e.g., customers who've never placed an order). The left side is always 'safe' — it's never excluded.
examples.realWorldUse: Find customers with NO orders (LEFT JOIN orders WHERE order_id IS NULL). Include all employees even if they have no manager. Show all products even if no inventory record exists. Any 'optional relationship' where the left entity can exist without the right. User reporting dashboards showing all users even those with zero activity.

examples.title: FULL OUTER JOIN — All Rows from Both Sides
examples.code:
-- MySQL doesn't support FULL OUTER JOIN directly.
-- Simulate with LEFT JOIN UNION RIGHT JOIN:
SELECT e.name, d.name AS department
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id

UNION

SELECT e.name, d.name AS department
FROM employees e
RIGHT JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id
WHERE e.id IS NULL;  -- only the unmatched right rows
examples.tableData: {"type":"join","joinType":"FULL","joinKey":{"left":"dept_id","right":"id"},"leftTable":{"name":"employees","columns":["id","name","dept_id"],"rows":[[1,"Alice",10],[2,"Bob",20],[3,"Dave",99]]},"rightTable":{"name":"departments","columns":["id","name"],"rows":[[10,"Engineering"],[20,"Marketing"],[30,"HR"]]},"matchedRows":[[0,0],[1,1]],"leftUnmatched":[2],"rightUnmatched":[2],"result":{"columns":["employee","department"],"rows":[["Alice","Engineering"],["Bob","Marketing"],["Dave",null],[null,"HR"]]},"explanation":"FULL OUTER JOIN: Alice+Bob matched. Dave kept (dept_id=99 no match → dept=NULL). HR kept (no employees → employee=NULL)."}
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":3,"lineCode":"LEFT JOIN part","variables":{"result":"Alice+Engineering, Bob+Marketing, Dave+NULL"},"phase":"LEFT","annotation":"LEFT JOIN gives all employees: Alice/Bob matched, Dave unmatched (dept=NULL)."},{"line":7,"lineCode":"UNION","variables":{"dedup":true},"phase":"UNION","annotation":"UNION combines results and removes duplicates. UNION ALL keeps duplicates."},{"line":8,"lineCode":"RIGHT JOIN WHERE e.id IS NULL","variables":{"new_rows":"HR only"},"phase":"RIGHT","annotation":"RIGHT JOIN gets all dept rows. WHERE e.id IS NULL keeps only those with no employee match: HR. Matched rows (Engineering,Marketing) already appear in the LEFT JOIN result."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A["LEFT JOIN\n+ all employees"] --> C["UNION"]
    B["RIGHT JOIN\nWHERE e.id IS NULL\n(unmatched depts only)"] --> C
    C --> D["Alice→Engineering"]
    C --> E["Bob→Marketing"]
    C --> F["Dave→NULL"]
    C --> G["NULL→HR"]
examples.pseudocode:
FULL OUTER JOIN = LEFT JOIN UNION RIGHT JOIN (MySQL)

-- Matches appear once (from LEFT JOIN)
-- Unmatched LEFT rows: left data + NULL right (from LEFT JOIN)
-- Unmatched RIGHT rows: NULL left + right data (from RIGHT JOIN WHERE left.pk IS NULL)

USE CASES:
- Reconciliation: find ALL differences between two tables
- Audit: which employees have no dept AND which depts have no employees
- Data migration: show all source and target rows, including gaps
examples.explanation: FULL OUTER JOIN returns ALL rows from BOTH tables. Matched rows appear once with data from both sides. Unmatched left rows show NULL for right columns; unmatched right rows show NULL for left columns. MySQL lacks native FULL OUTER JOIN — simulate it with LEFT JOIN UNION (RIGHT JOIN WHERE left.pk IS NULL). The second part of UNION adds only the unmatched right rows to avoid duplicating matched rows.
examples.realWorldUse: Data reconciliation between two systems (find records in System A but not B, and vice versa). Audit comparing expected vs actual. Inventory reconciliation: products ordered vs products received. Employee list from HR system vs payroll system — find discrepancies.

examples.title: Subquery with IN — Filter by Dynamic List
examples.code:
-- Find employees in departments with budget > 300000
-- Method 1: Subquery with IN
SELECT name, salary
FROM employees
WHERE dept_id IN (
    SELECT id
    FROM departments
    WHERE budget > 300000
);

-- Method 2: Equivalent JOIN
SELECT e.name, e.salary
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id
WHERE d.budget > 300000;
examples.tableData: {"type":"subquery","subquerySQL":"SELECT id FROM departments WHERE budget > 300000","subqueryResult":{"columns":["id"],"rows":[[10],[30]]},"outerTable":{"name":"employees","columns":["id","name","salary","dept_id"],"rows":[[1,"Alice",85000,10],[2,"Bob",55000,20],[3,"Carol",92000,10],[4,"Dave",70000,30]]},"filteredRows":[0,2,3],"result":{"columns":["name","salary"],"rows":[["Alice",85000],["Carol",92000],["Dave",70000]]},"note":"Subquery runs first → returns [10, 30]. Outer query then filters: dept_id IN (10, 30). Bob(dept_id=20) is excluded."}
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":5,"lineCode":"SELECT id FROM departments WHERE budget > 300000","variables":{"result":"[10, 30]"},"phase":"SUBQUERY","annotation":"Inner query runs first independently. Returns dept ids with budget > 300k: Engineering(10) and HR(30). Marketing(200k) excluded."},{"line":3,"lineCode":"WHERE dept_id IN ([10, 30])","variables":{"Alice":"dept_id=10 → IN → included","Bob":"dept_id=20 → NOT IN → excluded"},"phase":"OUTER","annotation":"Outer query uses the subquery result as a filter list. Alice(10) and Carol(10) and Dave(30) pass. Bob(20) excluded."},{"line":10,"lineCode":"-- Equivalent JOIN approach","variables":{"performance":"JOIN usually faster for large datasets"},"phase":"ALTERNATIVE","annotation":"JOIN approach: same result, often faster because the optimizer can use indexes on the join columns directly."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A["① Subquery runs first"] --> B["SELECT id FROM departments\nWHERE budget > 300000"]
    B --> C["Returns: [10, 30]"]
    C --> D["② Outer query uses result"]
    D --> E["WHERE dept_id IN (10, 30)"]
    E --> F["Alice(10) ✓, Carol(10) ✓, Dave(30) ✓"]
    E --> G["Bob(20) ✗"]
examples.pseudocode:
Subquery execution:
1. Inner query (subquery) runs FIRST, independently
2. Returns a result set
3. Outer query uses that result set

IN vs EXISTS:
  IN:     WHERE dept_id IN (SELECT id ...)   -- gets list, then checks membership
  EXISTS: WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM dept d WHERE d.id = e.dept_id AND budget > 300k)

IN is simpler. EXISTS is faster for large subquery results
because EXISTS short-circuits (stops after first match).

Correlated subquery (uses outer row's values):
  WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees e2
                   WHERE e2.dept_id = e.dept_id)  -- e.dept_id is from outer!
examples.explanation: Subqueries (inner queries) run first and pass their result to the outer query. IN checks membership in a list. Uncorrelated subqueries run once; correlated subqueries run once per outer row (expensive). JOIN is usually equivalent to a subquery but often faster — the query optimizer can choose better join algorithms with indexes. Use subqueries for readability when performance isn't critical.
examples.realWorldUse: Find products in high-revenue categories. Find users who bought at least one premium product. Find employees in offices with > 50 people. Any 'filter by criteria from another table' problem. Correlated subqueries: 'employees earning above their department's average' (classic interview question).

examples.title: Self Join — Compare a Table to Itself
examples.code:
-- Find employees earning more than their manager
SELECT
    e.name        AS employee,
    e.salary      AS emp_salary,
    m.name        AS manager,
    m.salary      AS mgr_salary
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m
    ON e.manager_id = m.id
WHERE e.salary > m.salary;
examples.tableData: {"type":"join","joinType":"INNER","joinKey":{"left":"manager_id","right":"id"},"leftTable":{"name":"employees (as e - employee)","columns":["id","name","salary","manager_id"],"rows":[[1,"Joe",70000,3],[2,"Henry",80000,4],[3,"Sam",60000,null],[4,"Max",90000,null]]},"rightTable":{"name":"employees (as m - manager)","columns":["id","name","salary"],"rows":[[3,"Sam",60000],[4,"Max",90000]]},"matchedRows":[[0,0],[1,1]],"leftUnmatched":[2,3],"rightUnmatched":[],"result":{"columns":["employee","emp_salary","manager","mgr_salary"],"rows":[["Joe",70000,"Sam",60000]]},"explanation":"Joe(70k) > Sam(60k) his manager → included. Henry(80k) < Max(90k) his manager → excluded. Sam and Max have no manager (NULL) → excluded by INNER JOIN."}
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":7,"lineCode":"FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id","variables":{"alias_e":"employee row","alias_m":"manager row"},"phase":"SELF_JOIN","annotation":"Same table, two aliases: 'e' = the employee side, 'm' = their manager (also in same table). ON links employee to their manager via manager_id."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"WHERE e.salary > m.salary","variables":{"Joe":"70000 > 60000(Sam) ✓","Henry":"80000 < 90000(Max) ✗"},"phase":"FILTER","annotation":"Joe earns more than his manager Sam → included. Henry earns less than Max → excluded."},{"line":1,"lineCode":"Sam and Max have manager_id=NULL","variables":{"excluded":true},"phase":"NULL","annotation":"Sam and Max are top-level managers (no manager_id). INNER JOIN excludes NULL join keys — they don't appear as employees."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A["employees table"] --> B["alias 'e' (employee view)"]
    A --> C["alias 'm' (manager view)"]
    B --> D["JOIN ON e.manager_id = m.id"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["Joe(e) ↔ Sam(m)"]
    D --> F["Henry(e) ↔ Max(m)"]
    E --> G["WHERE 70k > 60k ✓ → included"]
    F --> H["WHERE 80k > 90k ✗ → excluded"]
examples.pseudocode:
SELF JOIN = join a table to itself using two aliases

FROM employees e   -- alias 'e': the employee side
JOIN employees m   -- alias 'm': the manager side  
    ON e.manager_id = m.id  -- link employee to their manager

COMMON SELF JOIN PATTERNS:
  Org hierarchy:     ON emp.manager_id = mgr.id
  Consecutive rows:  ON l1.id + 1 = l2.id
  Pairs:             ON a.id < b.id AND a.category = b.category
  Comparisons:       WHERE a.value > b.value (find 'better than' records)
examples.explanation: A self join treats the same table as if it were two separate tables. Two aliases let you reference the table in two roles simultaneously. Essential for hierarchical data (employees + managers in same table), consecutive row comparisons (logging, sequences), and finding relationships within the same dataset (pairs of users who live in the same city).
examples.realWorldUse: Employee hierarchy (manager-subordinate in same table). Find students with the same score as another student. Detect consecutive events in logs. Find products with the same category. Friend recommendations (users with same interests). Org chart traversal. Any recursive relationship stored in a single table.

problems.title: Combine Two Tables
problems.description:
Report first name, last name, city, and state for each person regardless of whether an address exists.

**Table: Person** (personId, lastName, firstName)
**Table: Address** (addressId, personId, city, state)

**Example:**
Person = [[1,'Wang','Allen'],[2,'Alice','Bob']]
Address = [[1,2,'New York City','New York'],[2,3,'Leetcode','California']]
Output: [['Allen','Wang',null,null],['Bob','Alice','New York City','New York']]
problems.hint1: You want ALL persons even if they have no address. Which JOIN type keeps all left-table rows?
problems.hint2: LEFT JOIN Person with Address ON personId. Persons without an address will have NULL for city and state.
problems.hint3: SELECT p.firstName, p.lastName, a.city, a.state FROM Person p LEFT JOIN Address a ON p.personId = a.personId;
problems.starterCode:
SELECT p.firstName, p.lastName, a.city, a.state
FROM Person p
-- Join type that keeps all persons
problems.solutionCode:
SELECT p.firstName, p.lastName, a.city, a.state
FROM Person p
LEFT JOIN Address a
    ON p.personId = a.personId;
problems.editorial: LEFT JOIN keeps all Person rows. Address rows without a matching Person are ignored. Person rows without an Address get NULL for city/state.

problems.title: Customers Who Never Order
problems.description:
Find all customers who never placed any order.

**Table: Customers** (id, name)
**Table: Orders** (id, customerId)

**Example:**
Customers = [[1,'Joe'],[2,'Henry'],[3,'Sam'],[4,'Max']]
Orders = [[1,3],[2,1]]
Output: [['Henry'],['Max']]
problems.hint1: Use LEFT JOIN to include all customers. How do you find customers with NO matching order?
problems.hint2: LEFT JOIN + WHERE Orders.id IS NULL finds customers with no matching order row.
problems.hint3: SELECT c.name FROM Customers c LEFT JOIN Orders o ON c.id = o.customerId WHERE o.id IS NULL;
problems.starterCode:
SELECT c.name AS Customers
FROM Customers c
-- LEFT JOIN and filter for no orders
problems.solutionCode:
SELECT c.name AS Customers
FROM Customers c
LEFT JOIN Orders o ON c.id = o.customerId
WHERE o.id IS NULL;
problems.editorial: LEFT JOIN keeps all customers. WHERE o.id IS NULL filters to only those with no matching order. This is the 'anti-join' pattern: LEFT JOIN + WHERE right.pk IS NULL.

problems.title: Rising Temperature
problems.description:
Find all dates' ids with higher temperature than the previous day.

**Table: Weather** (id, recordDate, temperature)

**Example:**
Weather = [[1,'2015-01-01',10],[2,'2015-01-02',25],[3,'2015-01-03',20],[4,'2015-01-04',30]]
Output: [[2],[4]]
problems.hint1: You need to compare each row to the previous day's row. The previous day is recordDate - 1. How do you join a row to the previous day's row?
problems.hint2: Self-join: JOIN Weather w2 ON w1.recordDate = w2.recordDate + INTERVAL 1 DAY. This pairs each day with the day before.
problems.hint3: SELECT w1.id FROM Weather w1 JOIN Weather w2 ON w1.recordDate = w2.recordDate + INTERVAL 1 DAY WHERE w1.temperature > w2.temperature;
problems.starterCode:
SELECT w1.id
FROM Weather w1
-- Self join to get previous day, then filter
problems.solutionCode:
SELECT w1.id
FROM Weather w1
JOIN Weather w2
    ON w1.recordDate = DATEADD(w2.recordDate, INTERVAL 1 DAY)
WHERE w1.temperature > w2.temperature;
problems.editorial: Self-join on dates: pair each row with the row from the previous day using date arithmetic. WHERE w1.temperature > w2.temperature keeps only warmer days.

problems.title: Employee Bonus
problems.description:
Report the name and bonus amount for each employee whose bonus is less than 1000 OR who has no bonus record.

**Table: Employee** (empId, name, supervisor, salary)
**Table: Bonus** (empId, bonus)

**Example:**
Employee = [[3,'Brad',null,4000],[1,'John',3,10000],[2,'Dan',3,10000],[4,'Thomas',3,10000]]
Bonus = [[2,500],[4,2000]]
Output: [['Brad',null],['John',null],['Dan',500]]
problems.hint1: Some employees have no bonus record at all. Use LEFT JOIN to include employees without any bonus entry.
problems.hint2: LEFT JOIN Bonus. Then: employees with NULL bonus (no record) and those with bonus < 1000 both qualify.
problems.hint3: WHERE b.bonus < 1000 OR b.bonus IS NULL — the IS NULL catches employees with no bonus record.
problems.starterCode:
SELECT e.name, b.bonus
FROM Employee e
-- LEFT JOIN Bonus, then filter
problems.solutionCode:
SELECT e.name, b.bonus
FROM Employee e
LEFT JOIN Bonus b ON e.empId = b.empId
WHERE b.bonus < 1000
   OR b.bonus IS NULL;
problems.editorial: LEFT JOIN keeps employees without bonus records. WHERE b.bonus < 1000 OR b.bonus IS NULL: the OR handles both cases. Thomas has bonus=2000 → excluded.

problems.title: Second Highest Salary
problems.description:
Find the second highest distinct salary. Return null if no second highest salary exists.

**Table: Employee** (id, salary)

**Example:**
Employee = [[1,100],[2,200],[3,300]] → Output: 200
Employee = [[1,100]] → Output: null
problems.hint1: Find the maximum salary that is less than the overall maximum salary. How do you exclude the maximum using a subquery?
problems.hint2: Subquery: SELECT MAX(salary) FROM Employee WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM Employee). This finds the max of all salaries below the top.
problems.hint3: Wrap in an outer SELECT to return NULL when no second highest exists: SELECT (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM Employee WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM Employee)) AS SecondHighestSalary;
problems.starterCode:
SELECT
    -- Return null if no second highest
    AS SecondHighestSalary
problems.editorial: Inner subquery gets the maximum salary. Outer MAX finds the highest salary below that (= 2nd highest). Wrapping in SELECT returns NULL naturally when no second highest exists. DISTINCT handles ties.

problems.title: Managers with at Least 5 Direct Reports
problems.description:
Find managers with at least 5 direct reports.

**Table: Employee** (id, name, department, managerId)

**Example:**
Employee = [[101,'John',null],[102,'Dan',101],[103,'James',101],[104,'Amy',101],[105,'Anne',101],[106,'Ron',101]]
Output: [['John']]
problems.hint1: Count direct reports per manager using GROUP BY managerId. Filter managers with COUNT >= 5. Then join to get the manager's name.
problems.hint2: Subquery: SELECT managerId FROM Employee GROUP BY managerId HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5. Then join Employee to get the name.
problems.hint3: SELECT e.name FROM Employee e WHERE e.id IN (SELECT managerId FROM Employee GROUP BY managerId HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5);
problems.starterCode:
SELECT e.name
FROM Employee e
-- Subquery to find manager IDs with >= 5 reports
problems.solutionCode:
SELECT e.name
FROM Employee e
WHERE e.id IN (
    SELECT managerId
    FROM Employee
    WHERE managerId IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY managerId
    HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5
);
problems.editorial: Subquery: GROUP BY managerId, HAVING COUNT >= 5 gives manager IDs with enough reports. Outer query finds names for those IDs. WHERE managerId IS NOT NULL avoids grouping NULL managerId.

problems.title: Students and Examinations
problems.description:
Find the number of times each student attended each exam. Include all student-subject pairs even if the student never attended that subject's exam.

**Table: Students** (student_id, student_name)
**Table: Subjects** (subject_name)
**Table: Examinations** (student_id, subject_name)

**Example:**
Students=[[1,'Alice'],[2,'Bob']], Subjects=[['Math'],['Physics']]
Examinations=[[1,'Math'],[1,'Math'],[1,'Physics'],[2,'Math']]
Output: [[1,'Alice','Math',2],[1,'Alice','Physics',1],[2,'Bob','Math',1],[2,'Bob','Physics',0]]
problems.pattern: CROSS JOIN LEFT JOIN GROUP BY
problems.hint1: You need all combinations of students × subjects (even if no exam). What JOIN creates all combinations?
problems.hint2: CROSS JOIN Students and Subjects to get all pairs. Then LEFT JOIN Examinations and count matches.
problems.hint3: SELECT s.student_id, s.student_name, sub.subject_name, COUNT(e.student_id) AS attended_exams FROM Students s CROSS JOIN Subjects sub LEFT JOIN Examinations e ON s.student_id=e.student_id AND sub.subject_name=e.subject_name GROUP BY s.student_id, s.student_name, sub.subject_name ORDER BY student_id, subject_name;
problems.starterCode:
SELECT s.student_id, s.student_name, sub.subject_name,
       COUNT(e.student_id) AS attended_exams
FROM Students s
-- CROSS JOIN subjects, then LEFT JOIN examinations
problems.solutionCode:
SELECT
    s.student_id,
    s.student_name,
    sub.subject_name,
    COUNT(e.student_id) AS attended_exams
FROM Students s
CROSS JOIN Subjects sub
LEFT JOIN Examinations e
    ON s.student_id = e.student_id
    AND sub.subject_name = e.subject_name
GROUP BY s.student_id, s.student_name, sub.subject_name
ORDER BY s.student_id, sub.subject_name;
problems.editorial: CROSS JOIN creates all student-subject pairs (Cartesian product). LEFT JOIN Examinations adds attendance data. COUNT(e.student_id) counts non-NULL matches (0 when LEFT JOIN produces NULL = no attendance).

problems.title: Product Sales Analysis III
problems.description:
Find the first year each product was sold and its price and quantity in that year.

**Table: Sales** (sale_id, product_id, year, quantity, price)
**Table: Product** (product_id, product_name)

**Example:**
Sales=[[1,100,2008,10,5000],[2,100,2009,12,5000],[7,200,2011,15,9000]]
Output: [[100,2008,10,5000],[200,2011,15,9000]]
problems.hint1: You need the row with the minimum year per product. Subquery: find MIN(year) per product_id. Then join back to get quantity and price.
problems.hint2: WHERE (product_id, year) IN (SELECT product_id, MIN(year) FROM Sales GROUP BY product_id).
problems.hint3: Tuple IN: match both product_id and year against the subquery result simultaneously.
problems.starterCode:
SELECT product_id, year AS first_year, quantity, price
FROM Sales
-- Filter for the minimum year per product
problems.solutionCode:
SELECT product_id, year AS first_year, quantity, price
FROM Sales
WHERE (product_id, year) IN (
    SELECT product_id, MIN(year)
    FROM Sales
    GROUP BY product_id
);
problems.editorial: Subquery gets (product_id, MIN year) per product. Outer query matches both product_id AND year — tuple IN. This correctly handles multiple products with different first years.

problems.title: Average Selling Price
problems.description:
Find the average selling price for each product. Round to 2 decimal places.

**Table: Prices** (product_id, start_date, end_date, price) — price for product in a date range
**Table: UnitsSold** (product_id, purchase_date, units)

**Example:**
Prices=[[1,'2019-02-17','2019-02-28',5],[1,'2019-03-01','2019-03-22',20],[2,'2019-02-01','2019-02-20',15]]
UnitsSold=[[1,'2019-02-25',100],[1,'2019-03-01',15],[2,'2019-02-10',200]]
Output: [[1,6.96],[2,15.00]]
problems.hint1: Each sale needs the applicable price (where purchase_date falls within start_date and end_date). How do you join on a date range?
problems.hint2: JOIN Prices ON product_id matches AND purchase_date BETWEEN start_date AND end_date.
problems.hint3: Weighted average: SUM(price * units) / SUM(units) for each product.
problems.starterCode:
SELECT u.product_id,
       ROUND(SUM(p.price * u.units) / SUM(u.units), 2) AS average_price
FROM UnitsSold u
-- Join Prices using date range condition
problems.solutionCode:
SELECT
    u.product_id,
    ROUND(SUM(p.price * u.units) / SUM(u.units), 2) AS average_price
FROM UnitsSold u
JOIN Prices p
    ON u.product_id = p.product_id
    AND u.purchase_date BETWEEN p.start_date AND p.end_date
GROUP BY u.product_id;
problems.editorial: Range join: ON purchase_date BETWEEN start_date AND end_date finds the price active at the time of sale. Weighted average: SUM(price * units) / SUM(units). ROUND to 2 decimals.

problems.title: Last Person to Fit in the Bus
problems.description:
A queue of people are getting on a bus. The bus has a weight limit of 1000kg. Find the person_name of the last person who can fit on the bus without exceeding 1000kg.

**Table: Queue** (person_id, person_name, weight, turn)

**Example:**
Queue=[[5,'Alice',250,1],[4,'Bob',175,5],[3,'Alex',350,2],[6,'John Cena',400,3],[1,'Winston',500,6],[2,'Marie',200,4]]
Output: [['John Cena']]
problems.hint1: Sort people by their turn order. Compute cumulative weight up to each person. Find the last person where cumulative weight <= 1000.
problems.hint2: Use a self-join to compute running sum: for person at turn T, sum weights of all persons with turn <= T.
problems.hint3: SELECT q1.person_name FROM Queue q1 JOIN Queue q2 ON q1.turn >= q2.turn GROUP BY q1.turn, q1.person_name HAVING SUM(q2.weight) <= 1000 ORDER BY q1.turn DESC LIMIT 1;
problems.starterCode:
SELECT q1.person_name
FROM Queue q1
-- Self join for running sum, filter <= 1000, take last
problems.solutionCode:
SELECT q1.person_name
FROM Queue q1
JOIN Queue q2 ON q1.turn >= q2.turn
GROUP BY q1.person_id, q1.person_name, q1.turn
HAVING SUM(q2.weight) <= 1000
ORDER BY q1.turn DESC
LIMIT 1;
problems.editorial: Self-join: for each person q1 at turn T, join all q2 with turn <= T (everyone boarding before or at the same time). SUM(q2.weight) = cumulative weight at that point. HAVING <= 1000 filters valid boarding points. ORDER BY turn DESC LIMIT 1 takes the last valid one.
